statement: [arXiv:2304.08000 Proposition 4.1] Let M be a finite simple matroid. Write L(M) for its lattice of flats and L(M)^op for the same lattice with the order reversed. Say M is modular if for all flats X,Y one has r_M(X)+r_M(Y)=r_M(X∧Y)+r_M(X∨Y). An adjoint of M means a matroid N with r(N)=r(M) together with an injective order-reversing map φ:L(M)→L(N) sending the hyperplanes of M bijectively onto the atoms of L(N). Then M is modular if and only if M has an adjoint N such that L(N) is isomorphic to L(M)^op.
proof_sketch: If M is modular, L(M)^op is again geometric: flats are intersections of hyperplanes so the opposite is atomic, and modularity gives semimodularity in the opposite. Hence it is the flat lattice of a simple matroid N of same rank; the identity on the underlying set of flats, viewed as a map L(M)→L(M)^op, is order-reversing and carries coatoms to atoms, so N is an adjoint. Conversely, if an adjoint N has L(N)≅L(M)^op, then L(M)^op is geometric. Since L(M) is geometric, both L(M) and its opposite are semimodular. Let ρ be the rank function of L(M), with total rank r. Semimodularity in L(M) gives ρ(X)+ρ(Y) ≥ ρ(X∧Y)+ρ(X∨Y). In L(M)^op the rank is r−ρ, and semimodularity gives (r−ρ(X))+(r−ρ(Y)) ≥ (r−ρ(X∧Y))+(r−ρ(X∨Y)), i.e. the reverse inequality. Thus equality holds for all X,Y, so M is modular. In applications, any order-reversing self-isomorphism of a flat lattice makes L(M)^op geometric, triggering this criterion after simplification.

statement: [arXiv:2304.08000 Theorem 4.3] Let M be a finite simple matroid such that every pair of flats X,Y is modular, i.e. r_M(X)+r_M(Y)=r_M(X∧Y)+r_M(X∨Y). Then M has only one adjoint up to matroid isomorphism. More precisely, if N is any adjoint of M, meaning r(N)=r(M) and there is an injective order-reversing map φ:L(M)→L(N) sending hyperplanes of M bijectively to atoms of L(N), then L(N) is isomorphic to L(M)^op.
proof_sketch: Use the Crapo-Cheung extension lattice E(M): its elements are linear subclasses A⊆H(M), i.e. subsets of hyperplanes such that whenever H1,H2∈A and r(H1∩H2)=r(M)−2, every hyperplane containing H1∩H2 also lies in A. Cheung's characterization embeds L(N) of any adjoint N into E(M), and the adjoint map embeds L(M)^op into L(N). Lemma 4.2 identifies E(M) with L(M)^op when M is modular via λ(X)=H_X={H hyperplane of M: X⊆H}. The map λ is order-preserving from the opposite order, injective, and H_X is linear because distinct hyperplanes containing X have corank-2 intersection by modularity, so any hyperplane through that intersection contains X. Surjectivity is the hard part: for a linear subclass A, set X_A=∩_{H∈A}H. One must prove every hyperplane H0 containing X_A is in A. If not, choose H1,...,H_{r−k}∈A whose successive intersections form a maximal chain down to X_A, and take the least i0 with ∩_{j<i0}H_j not contained in H0 but ∩_{j≤i0}H_j contained in H0. The intersection property of modular geometric lattices applied in L(M)^op produces a new hyperplane H_{0i0} containing H_{i0}∩H0 and ∩_{j<i0}H_j. If H_{0i0} were in A, linearity of A would force H0∈A; hence H_{0i0} is another counterexample, but with a smaller minimal index. Iterating gives an infinite descent in a finite interval, contradiction. Thus λ is onto. Therefore L(M)^op embeds into L(N), and L(N) embeds into E(M)≅L(M)^op; finiteness forces both embeddings to be onto, so L(N)≅L(M)^op. Since simple matroids are determined by their flat lattices, all adjoints are isomorphic.

statement: [arXiv:2304.08000 Proposition 4.4] Let M be a finite simple matroid. Then M is modular, i.e. every pair of flats X,Y satisfies r_M(X)+r_M(Y)=r_M(X∧Y)+r_M(X∨Y), if and only if every connected component of M is either the one-element rank-one free matroid U_{1,1} or the matroid of a finite projective geometry. Here a finite projective geometry means a finite point-line incidence geometry in which every two distinct points lie on exactly one line, every line contains at least three points, and the Veblen axiom holds: if a,b,c,d are four distinct points with no three collinear and the line ab meets the line cd, then the line ac meets the line bd; the associated simple matroid has the points as ground set and flats equal to subspaces, i.e. subsets closed under taking the whole line through any two of their points.
proof_sketch: This is the classical Birkhoff characterization of finite modular geometric lattices. Matroid direct sum corresponds to direct product of flat lattices; ranks, meets, and joins decompose componentwise, so modularity is equivalent to modularity of each connected component. A rank-one simple connected component is U_{1,1}. For a connected simple modular component of rank at least two, take atoms as points and rank-two flats as lines. Axiom p1 is the matroid fact that two distinct points span a unique rank-two flat. Axiom p2 follows from connectedness plus modularity: if the line ℓ=x∨y had only x,y, choose a circuit C containing x and y; with F=cl(C−{x,y}), modularity of ℓ and F gives r(ℓ∧F)=1, while circuit minimality keeps x,y∉F, producing a third point on ℓ. The Veblen axiom is exactly the rank computation in a modular lattice: if lines ab and cd meet, then their join has rank 3, and within that rank-3 modular interval the two lines ac and bd have meet of rank 1, hence meet in a point. Thus each nontrivial connected component is a finite projective geometry. Conversely, the subspace lattice of a finite projective geometry is a modular geometric lattice, and direct sums/products of such components, together with U_{1,1} factors, remain modular.

statement: [arXiv:math/0410455 Proposition 2.12 (with the preceding dualization construction)] Let \(p=(p_I)_{I\in \binom{[n]}{d}}\) be a tropical Pluecker vector, let \(D_p\) be its regular matroidal subdivision of the hypersimplex \(\Delta(d,n)\), and let \(L(p)\subset \mathbb{R}^n\) be the corresponding \(d\)-dimensional tropical linear space. Define a vector \(p^\perp\) on \((n-d)\)-subsets by \(p^\perp_K=p_{[n]\setminus K}\). Then \(p^\perp\) is a tropical Pluecker vector. The subdivision \(D_{p^\perp}\) of \(\Delta(n-d,n)\) is the image of \(D_p\) under the affine map \((x_1,\ldots,x_n)\mapsto (1-x_1,\ldots,1-x_n)\); under this map each matroid polytope \(P_M\) is sent to the polytope of the dual matroid \(P_{M^*}\). Writing \(L^\perp:=L(p^\perp)\), the assignment \(L\mapsto L^\perp\) is an inclusion-reversing bijection, in fact an involution, on the set of tropical linear spaces in \(\mathbb{R}^n\). The bounded subcomplex of \(L^\perp\) is the negative of the bounded subcomplex of \(L\), under \(w\mapsto -w\) modulo the lineality space \(\mathbb{R}(1,\ldots,1)\). Consequently, if \(L\) is a \(d\)-dimensional tropical linear space in \(n\)-space, then the bounded part of \(L\) has dimension at most \(\min(d,n-d)\).
proof_sketch: The complement map identifies vertices \(e_I\in\Delta(d,n)\) with \(e_{[n]\setminus I}\in\Delta(n-d,n)\). Since the height assigned to \(e_{[n]\setminus I}\) by \(p^\perp\) is exactly the height assigned to \(e_I\) by \(p\), the lower regular subdivision is carried to the image subdivision. A subset of vertices is the set of bases of a matroid \(M\) iff the complementary subsets are the bases of the dual matroid \(M^*\); hence all cells remain matroidal, so by Proposition 2.2 \(p^\perp\) satisfies the tropical Pluecker relations. Applying the complement operation twice recovers \(p\), and the defining tropical linear equations/valuated-matroid duality give the inclusion reversal \(L_1\subseteq L_2\Rightarrow L_2^\perp\subseteq L_1^\perp\). For the bounded-part statement, let \(M_w\) be the matroid labelling the face of \(D_p\) where \(p_I-\sum_{i\in I}w_i\) is minimized. For \(p^\perp\) at \(-w\), the minimizing matroid is \(M_w^*\), since \(p_{[n]\setminus K}+\sum_{i\in K}w_i=p_I-\sum_{i\in I}w_i+\sum_i w_i\). Proposition 2.10 says a face of \(L\) is bounded exactly when \(M_w\) is both loop-free and co-loop-free. This condition is self-dual, because loops and co-loops are interchanged by matroid duality. Thus bounded faces correspond under \(w\mapsto -w\). The dimension bound follows because the same bounded complex sits inside both the \(d\)-dimensional space \(L\) and the \((n-d)\)-dimensional space \(L^\perp\).

statement: [arXiv:math/0410455 Proposition 3.1 (with Corollary 3.2)] Let \(L=L(p)\) and \(L'=L(p')\) be tropical linear spaces in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) of dimensions \(d\) and \(d'\), with \(k=d+d'-n\ge 0\). For each \(J\in \binom{[n]}{k}\), define \[ q_J=\min_{\substack{I\in\binom{[n]}d,\ I'\in\binom{[n]}{d'}\\ I\cap I'=J}}\bigl(p_I+p'_{I'}\bigr). \] The conditions force \(I\cup I'=[n]\). Then \(q=(q_J)_{J\in\binom{[n]}k}\) is a tropical Pluecker vector. The tropical linear space \(L(q)\) is denoted \(L\cap_{\mathrm{stable}}L'\), the stable intersection of \(L\) and \(L'\). Moreover, if \(D,D'\), and \(E\) are the regular matroidal subdivisions of \(\Delta(d,n),\Delta(d',n)\), and \(\Delta(k,n)\) induced by \(p,p'\), and \(q\), respectively, then every face of \(E\) is of the form \(P_{M\wedge M'}\) for faces \(P_M\in D\) and \(P_{M'}\in D'\), where \(M\wedge M'\) is matroid intersection: its spanning sets are the intersections \(U\cap U'\), with \(U\) spanning \(M\) and \(U'\) spanning \(M'\). If \(\operatorname{rank}(M\wedge M')>k\), the corresponding cell is empty; if \(\operatorname{rank}(M\wedge M')=k\), the corresponding cell is exactly \(P_{M\wedge M'}\).
proof_sketch: Let \(P\subset\mathbb{R}\times\Delta(d,n)\) and \(P'\subset\mathbb{R}\times\Delta(d',n)\) be the polyhedra above the lower convex hulls of the lifted vertices \((p_I,e_I)\) and \((p'_{I'},e_{I'})\). Embed \(\Delta(k,n)\) in \(\Delta(d,n)+\Delta(d',n)\) by \(\iota(e_J)=e_J+(1,\ldots,1)\). Intersect the Minkowski sum \(P+P'\) with \(\mathbb{R}\times\iota(\Delta(k,n))\). The lower faces of this intersection project to exactly the regular subdivision induced by the coordinates \(q_J\), since the minimum height over decompositions \(\iota(e_J)=e_I+e_{I'}\) is precisely the displayed minimum. Thus every cell of the subdivision of \(\Delta(k,n)\) has the form \(R=\iota(\Delta(k,n))\cap(P_M+P_{M'})\) for some cells \(P_M\subset D\), \(P_{M'}\subset D'\). The vertices of \(R\) are the points \(\iota(e_J)\) with \(J=I\cap I'\), where \(I,I'\) are bases of \(M,M'\) and \(I\cup I'=[n]\). Matroid intersection says these \(J\)'s are precisely the bases of \(M\wedge M'\) when \(\operatorname{rank}(M\wedge M')=k\); if the rank is larger, no such pairs of bases occur, so the intersection is empty. Hence every nonempty cell of the subdivision induced by \(q\) is a matroid polytope. By Proposition 2.2, a regular subdivision of a hypersimplex whose cells are matroidal is equivalent to the tropical Pluecker relations; therefore \(q\) is a tropical Pluecker vector.

statement: [arXiv:math/0410455 Proposition 3.4 and Theorem 3.6] Let \(L\) and \(L'\) be tropical linear spaces in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) of dimensions \(d\) and \(d'\), with \(d+d'\ge n\), and let \(L\cap_{\mathrm{stable}}L'\) be the stable intersection defined by the tropical Pluecker-coordinate formula above. Then \(L\cap_{\mathrm{stable}}L'\subseteq L\cap L'\). If \(L\) and \(L'\) meet transversely, meaning that whenever faces of \(L\) and \(L'\) meet their affine spans together span \(\mathbb{R}^n\), then \(L\cap_{\mathrm{stable}}L'=L\cap L'\). Furthermore, for a generic vector \(v\in\mathbb{R}^n\), the spaces \(L\) and \(L'+v\) meet transversely, and \[ L\cap_{\mathrm{stable}}L'=\lim_{v\to 0}\bigl(L\cap(L'+v)\bigr), \] where the limit is taken through generic translations, equivalently through the tropical linear spaces determined by their continuously varying Pluecker coordinates.
proof_sketch: For a point \(w\), let \(P_M,P_{M'},P_N\) be the cells selected by \(w\) in the subdivisions for \(L,L'\), and \(L\cap_{stable}L'\). Proposition 3.1 gives \(P_N=(P_M+P_{M'})\cap\iota(\Delta(d+d'-n,n))\), equivalently \(N=M\wedge M'\) when nonempty. By Proposition 2.3, \(w\in L\) iff \(M\) is loop-free, and similarly for \(L'\) and for the stable intersection. If an element \(e\) is a loop of \(M\), then the \(e\)-coordinate is \(0\) on \(P_M\); after intersecting with \(\iota(\Delta)\), the corresponding coordinate in \(P_N\) is forced to be \(0\), so \(e\) is a loop of \(N\). Thus loop-freeness of \(N\) forces loop-freeness of both \(M\) and \(M'\), proving \(L\cap_{stable}L'\subseteq L\cap L'\). Conversely assume the faces through \(w\) meet transversely and \(M,M'\) are loop-free. Decompose \(M=\bigoplus_{i\in I}M_i\) and \(M'=\bigoplus_{i\in I'}M'_i\). Form the bipartite graph \(\Gamma(M,M')\) with vertices \(I\sqcup I'\) and one edge for each ground-set element lying in the corresponding pair of connected components. The affine span of \(M^\vee\) is cut out by equations \(x_a-x_b=\mathrm{constant}\) within each component, and similarly for \((M')^\vee\); transversality gives a dimension count forcing \(\Gamma\) to be a forest without multiple edges. Lemma 3.3 then applies: for such transverse matroids, \(M\wedge M'\) has rank \(d+d'-n\) and is loop-free. Hence \(N\) is loop-free and \(w\in L\cap_{stable}L'\), giving equality in the transverse case. For the limit statement, there are finitely many pairs of faces \((F,F')\). If their affine spans fail to span \(\mathbb{R}^n\), the translations \(v\) for which \(F\cap(F'+v)\neq\varnothing\) lie in a proper polyhedral subset. Avoiding all these subsets gives generic transverse intersection. Translation by \(v\) changes Pluecker coordinates by \(p'_{I}(L'+v)=p'_I(L')+\sum_{i\in I}v_i\). Therefore stable-intersection coordinates are finite minima of affine functions of \(v\), hence vary continuously. For generic \(v\), the transverse equality just proved gives \(L\cap(L'+v)=L\cap_{stable}(L'+v)\); letting \(v\to0\) yields the displayed limit.

statement: [arXiv:2005.13727 Theorem B] Let T=R∪{∞} with the min-plus convention, and let P(T^[n])=(T^[n]\{(∞,...,∞)})/R·1 be tropical projective space. Let μ be a valuated matroid on ground set [n], with underlying matroid M and loop set ℓ⊆[n]. For S⊆[n], let μ/S denote contraction by S, viewed as a valuated matroid on [n]\S; write trop(μ/S) for the usual tropical linear space in R^[n]\S/R·1, and place it in P(T^[n]) by setting the coordinates in S equal to ∞. Let μ* be the dual valuated matroid. If μ has rank r, its valuated circuits are the non-all-∞ vectors C_μ(A)∈T^[n], for A∈C([n],r+1), defined by C_μ(A)_i=μ(A\{i}) for i∈A and C_μ(A)_i=∞ for i∉A; valuated cocircuits are valuated circuits of μ*. Then the following five subsets of P(T^[n]) are equal: (i) the projective tropical linear space of μ, defined by trop(μ)=⋃_{∅⊆S⊊[n]}(trop(μ/S)×{∞}^S); (ii) the projective tropical prevariety cut out by the valuated circuits of μ, namely the set of u∈P(T^[n]) such that for every valuated circuit C of μ the minimum of {C_i+u_i : i∈[n]} is achieved at least twice, with the paper's convention that an all-∞ list counts as achieving the minimum at least twice; (iii) the union of coloopless cells in the closure inside P(T^[n]) of the dual complex of μ*, namely the set of u such that the face Δ^u_{μ*} selected by u is the base polytope of a coloopless matroid, where for boundary points u this face is interpreted via the induced subdivision on the corresponding restriction; (iv) the tropical span of the valuated cocircuits of μ, i.e. the projectivizations of all finite tropical linear combinations min(a_1+C^*_1,...,a_m+C^*_m) with C^*_j valuated cocircuits and a_j∈R; and (v) the closure in P(T^[n]) of trop(μ/ℓ)×{∞}^ℓ.
proof_sketch: The equality (ii)=(iv) is the valuated circuit/cocircuit theorem of Murota-Tamura: vectors satisfying all circuit tropical hyperplanes are exactly the tropical span of cocircuits. For (i)=(iii), start from the standard affine characterization of a tropical linear space: for any valuated matroid η, trop(η) in the dense torus equals the set of u for which the face Δ^u_{η*} in the coherent subdivision of the dual valuated matroid is coloopless. Apply this on every stratum of P(T^[n]). The needed compatibility with strata is Theorem 2.3.8: for an M-convex weight, the closure of its dual complex meets the stratum with finite support A in the dual complex of the restriction to A. Together with (μ/S)*=μ*|([n]\S), this identifies the stratum of the coloopless-cell set with trop(μ/S). For (iii)⊆(v), first note that if a boundary stratum keeps a loop of M finite, then the corresponding contraction has a loop, hence contributes no coloopless dual cells; thus only strata in the closure of the loop-deleted part can occur. Reducing to M loopless, take a boundary point u with finite support A satisfying the coloopless-cell condition. Choose u′ in trop(μ|([n]\A)), possible because looplessness is inherited by restrictions needed here. Points u×c u′ in the dense torus tend to u as c→∞. Lemma 2.3.4 says that near the boundary the relevant initial face is computed from the face of μ* in direction e_[n]\A, and Lemma 2.3.7 factors this face as μ*|A × μ*/A. The two factors are coloopless by the assumptions on u and u′, so u×c u′ lies in trop(μ) for large c; hence u is in its projective closure. For (v)⊆(ii), after deleting loops, the dense-torus equality between circuit prevariety and tropical linear space is Proposition 3.2.1, and projective tropical prevarieties are closed. Finally, for (ii)⊆(i), restrict to the stratum whose ∞-coordinates are S. In every circuit equation, terms with coordinates in S disappear. Theorem 3.1.6 identifies the valuated circuits of μ/S as the minimal-support projections of valuated circuits of μ, so satisfying all original projective circuit equations implies satisfying the usual circuit equations for μ/S, hence the point lies in trop(μ/S)×{∞}^S.

statement: [arXiv:2005.13727 Proposition 4.2.3] Fix integers 0≤r≤s≤n. Let μ∈T^C([n],r) and ν∈T^C([n],s) be non-all-∞ vectors, considered up to adding constants in their respective projective factors. The flag Dressian FlDr(r,s;n) is the multi-projective tropical prevariety in P(T^C([n],r))×P(T^C([n],s)) cut out by the rank-r and rank-s tropical Grassmann-Plücker relations and by the tropical incidence-Plücker relations ⊕_{j′∈J′\I′} P_{I′∪{j′}} ⊙ P_{J′\{j′}}, for I′∈C([n],r−1) and J′∈C([n],s+1). Then (μ,ν) lies in FlDr(r,s;n) if and only if μ and ν are valuated matroids and μ is a valuated matroid quotient of ν. Explicitly, if M and N are the underlying matroids of μ and ν, this quotient condition μ↞ν means: for every I∈B(M), J∈B(N), and i∈I\J, there exists j∈J\I such that μ(I)+ν(J) ≥ μ(I\{i}∪{j})+ν(J\{j}∪{i}). More generally, points of FlDr(r_1,...,r_k;n) are exactly valuated flag matroids (μ_1,...,μ_k), meaning that each μ_i is a valuated matroid and μ_i↞μ_j for all i<j.
proof_sketch: The tropical Grassmann-Plücker relations for μ and ν separately are equivalent to μ and ν being valuated matroids, by the standard Dressian/valuated-matroid characterization. It remains to interpret the tropical incidence relations. Fix I′ of size r−1 and J′ of size s+1, and choose an element i∈J′\I′. Set I=I′∪{i} and J=J′\{i}. Then the incidence tropical polynomial ⊕_{j′∈J′\I′} P_{I′∪{j′}}⊙P_{J′\{j′}} has one term P_I⊙P_J and the remaining terms are exactly P_{I\{i}∪{j}}⊙P_{J\{j}∪{i}} for j∈J\I. Evaluating at (μ,ν), the condition that the minimum is attained at least twice is equivalent to saying that no term is a strict unique minimum. Applying this with each possible distinguished i says precisely that for each finite term μ(I)+ν(J) and each i∈I\J there is some j∈J\I with μ(I)+ν(J) ≥ μ(I−i+j)+ν(J−j+i), which is the valuated quotient exchange inequality. Conversely, if all these quotient inequalities hold, then in every incidence polynomial each term has another term of value no larger; hence no term can be uniquely minimal, so the tropical incidence relation holds. For k factors, the defining equations are the Grassmann-Plücker equations for each factor and the pairwise incidence-Plücker equations for each i<j, so the same argument gives exactly the pairwise valuated quotient conditions.

statement: [arXiv:2005.13727 Theorem 4.3.1] Let μ and ν be valuated matroids on the same ground set [n], of ranks r and s with r≤s. Let trop(μ), trop(ν)⊆P(T^[n]) be their projective tropical linear spaces as in Theorem B. Then μ is a valuated matroid quotient of ν, denoted μ↞ν, if and only if trop(μ)⊆trop(ν). Equivalently, for a sequence μ=(μ_1,...,μ_k) of valuated matroids on [n] with weakly increasing ranks, μ is a valuated flag matroid, i.e. μ_i↞μ_j for every i<j, if and only if the corresponding projective tropical linear spaces form a flag trop(μ_1)⊆...⊆trop(μ_k).
proof_sketch: Use the two dual descriptions of projective tropical linear spaces from Theorem B. First, trop(μ) is the tropical span of the valuated cocircuits C*(μ). Since tropical linear spaces are closed under tropical linear combinations, trop(μ)⊆trop(ν) is equivalent to every valuated cocircuit of μ lying in trop(ν). Second, trop(ν) is the projective tropical prevariety cut out by the valuated circuits of ν. Thus a cocircuit C^*_μ(I′), indexed by I′∈C([n],r−1), lies in trop(ν) exactly when for every circuit C_ν(J′), indexed by J′∈C([n],s+1), the minimum of the numbers C_ν(J′)_j + C^*_μ(I′)_j over j∈[n] is achieved at least twice. By the definitions of valuated circuits and cocircuits, the finite entries in this list are precisely μ(I′∪{j}) + ν(J′\{j}) for j∈J′\I′; all other entries are ∞. Therefore the condition for all I′ and J′ is exactly the tropical incidence-Plücker condition ⊕_{j∈J′\I′} P_{I′∪{j}}⊙P_{J′\{j}}. Proposition 4.2.3 identifies these tropical incidence-Plücker conditions with the valuated quotient condition μ↞ν. Applying this pairwise to a sequence gives the flag statement.

statement: [arXiv:2412.12059 Theorem 4.7] Let M be a simple matroid, and let L₁(M) denote the set of hyperplanes of M. A subset H of L₁(M) is called a linear subclass if, whenever H_i,H_j∈H and H_i,H_j cover their intersection H_i∩H_j, then every hyperplane of M covering H_i∩H_j also lies in H. Let Dr_s^1(M) be the simplified elementary relative Dressian of M: it is the image of Dr^1(M), the space of codimension-one tropical linear subspaces of Trop M, in tropical projective space with coordinates indexed by L₁(M), obtained by recording one coordinate for each hyperplane; equivalently, in the ordinary matroid case its torus part is cut out by the equations that min{x(H₁),x(H₂),x(H₃)} is attained at least twice for every concurrent triple of hyperplanes H₁,H₂,H₃, i.e. three distinct hyperplanes meeting in a corank-two flat. For H⊂L₁(M), put e_H = ∑_{H∈H} e_{H} in R^{L₁(M)}. Then the cones cone(e_{H₁},…,e_{H_k}) + R·1, where ∅⊊H₁⊊⋯⊊H_k⊊L₁(M) runs over all strict chains of proper nonempty linear subclasses, form a simplicial fan, and the support of this fan is exactly Dr_s^1(M)^∘ := Dr_s^1(M)∩T^{L₁(M)}. Equivalently, the torus part of the simplified elementary relative Dressian is the order-complex fan of the proper part of the elementary quotient lattice cQt^1(M), via the standard identification of elementary quotients with linear subclasses.
proof_sketch: Use the preceding description of Dr_s^1(M) by concurrent-triple tropical hyperplanes. First take a cone associated to a chain of linear subclasses and a point w=a₁e_{H₁}+⋯+a_ke_{H_k} with a_i≥0. For any concurrent triple {K₁,K₂,K₃}, the linear-subclass axiom severely restricts the membership pattern of the K_i in the chain: either none have entered, only one has entered, or once two have entered at some level the third is forced to enter at the same or earlier level. Translating this into coordinates of w shows the minimum of w(K₁),w(K₂),w(K₃) is always attained at least twice, so the whole cone lies in Dr_s^1(M)^∘. Conversely, take w∈Dr_s^1(M)^∘. After adding a scalar multiple of 1, write w uniquely as a positive linear combination of characteristic vectors of nested superlevel sets, w=a₁e_{G₁}+⋯+a_ke_{G_k}, with a_i>0 and ∅⊊G₁⊊⋯⊊G_k=L₁(M), up to dropping the final full-set term as lineality. The tropical equations force every G_i to be a linear subclass: if K₁,K₂∈G_i are two members of a concurrent triple, compare w(K₁),w(K₂),w(K₃); the condition that the minimum among the three is achieved twice implies K₃ has coordinate large enough to also belong to G_i. Thus every point lies in one of the stated cones. The uniqueness of the nested-level-set decomposition gives that intersections of cones are common faces, hence the cones form a simplicial fan.

statement: [arXiv:2412.12059 Proposition 4.12] Let M and W be simple matroids of the same rank d. The following conditions are equivalent. (1) The ground set of W is L₁(M), the set of hyperplanes of M, and every concurrent triple of hyperplanes of M is a circuit of W. (2) There is an order-reversing embedding L(W) → cQt^1(M), where cQt^1(M)=Qt^1(M)∪{M} is the elementary quotient lattice of M, such that the rank-one flats of W, identified with elements of its ground set L₁(M), map onto the corresponding principal elementary quotients associated to the hyperplanes of M. (3) The ground set of W is L₁(M) and Trop W is contained in Dr_s^1(M), the simplified elementary relative Dressian of M. (4) W is an adjoint of M, i.e. W is a rank-d matroid on L₁(M) such that L(M) embeds order-reversingly into L(W) in the usual sense of adjoint geometries.
proof_sketch: The equivalence between (2) and (4) is the standard adjoint/extension equivalence: (2)⇒(4) is attributed to Bachem–Kern, and (4)⇒(2) to Cheung's embedding theorem. For (1)⇒(2), identify cQt^1(M) with the lattice of linear subclasses of M, ordered oppositely by inclusion. For a flat F of W define ι(F) to be the linear subclass generated by the atoms H of W with H≤_W F; since the atoms of W are hyperplanes of M, this is a subset of L₁(M). The hypothesis that every concurrent triple of M is a circuit of W implies this subset is already closed under the linear-subclass axiom: if two hyperplanes in it occur in a concurrent triple, the circuit relation forces the third atom into the same flat F. Thus ι(F) is a linear subclass. If F₁≤F₂ in L(W), then ι(F₁)⊂ι(F₂), which is order reversal after passing to cQt^1(M); injectivity follows because a flat of a simple matroid is determined by its atoms. For (2)⇒(3), apply Theorem 4.7: the Bergman fan Trop W is the order-complex fan of the proper flat lattice of W, and the order-reversing embedding sends its chains to chains of linear subclasses, hence into the fan Dr_s^1(M). For (3)⇒(1), each concurrent-triple equation min{x(H₁),x(H₂),x(H₃)} is a tropical hyperplane containing Trop W. For the Bergman fan of an ordinary matroid, such containment forces {H₁,H₂,H₃} to be dependent in W; because W is simple, a dependent three-element set is a circuit. Hence every concurrent triple is a circuit.

statement: [arXiv:2412.12059 Proposition 4.14] Let W be an adjoint of a simple rank-d matroid M. Thus W is a rank-d matroid on the set L₁(M) of hyperplanes of M, with an order-reversing adjoint embedding α:L(M)→L(W). Then: (1) If B is a basis of M, the d hyperplanes cl_M(B−i), for i∈B, form a basis of W. (2) If F is a flat of W, then C_F := {G∈L(M) : α(G)≤F in L(W)} is a modular cut of M; equivalently, it is a nonempty order filter in L(M) such that whenever G₁,G₂∈C_F form a modular pair in M, their intersection G₁∩G₂ also belongs to C_F. (3) If M is modular, i.e. its lattice of flats is a modular lattice, then the adjoint W is unique and there are lattice isomorphisms L(M) ≅ cQt^1(M) ≅ L(W)^op. In particular, the simplified elementary relative Dressian Dr_s^1(M) is isomorphic, as the corresponding Bergman/order-complex tropical linear space, to Trop M.
proof_sketch: For (1), use the adjoint embedding α. A basis B={b₁,…,b_d} of M gives hyperplanes H_i=cl_M(B−b_i). In the geometry of adjoints, these hyperplanes are atoms of W, and joins of such atoms correspond under α to intersections of the corresponding hyperplanes of M. The full intersection ⋂_i H_i is the bottom flat of M, so the d atoms H_i span W. Since rk W=d, they form a basis. For (2), α is order-reversing, so if G∈C_F and G≤G′ in L(M), then α(G′)≤α(G)≤F; hence C_F is an order filter. If G₁,G₂∈C_F are a modular pair, the adjoint embedding identifies the image of their meet with the join of their images, α(G₁∩G₂)=α(G₁)∨α(G₂), for modular pairs. Since both α(G_i) lie below F and F is a flat of W, their join lies below F, so G₁∩G₂∈C_F. Thus C_F is a modular cut. For (3), in a modular matroid every pair of flats is modular, so a modular cut is simply an order filter closed under pairwise meet. Finite meet-closure gives a unique minimal element, hence every modular cut is principal. Therefore every linear subclass is the set of hyperplanes containing a unique flat of M, and the elementary quotient lattice cQt^1(M) is exactly the lattice of principal truncations, identified with L(M). The adjoint flat lattice is therefore forced to be L(M)^op with the prescribed ground-set identification by hyperplanes, so W is unique. Finally, Theorem 4.7 identifies Dr_s^1(M) with the order-complex/Bergman fan of cQt^1(M); under cQt^1(M)≅L(M), this is Trop M.

statement: [arXiv:2204.04658 Theorem 2.4] Let F be a tract equipped with a fixed involution, let E be a finite ground set, and let M_1,...,M_s be F-matroids on E. For an F-matroid P, write C(P) for its set of F-circuits, P^* for its dual, C^*(P)=C(P^*) for its set of F-cocircuits, and V^*(P)=C(P)^⊥ for its set of F-covectors, i.e. the set of all vectors in F^E orthogonal to every circuit of P under the paper's inner product. For two F-matroids A of rank r and B of rank w on E, write B ։ A if A is a quotient of B, meaning that for any Grassmann-Plücker functions μ:E^r→F and ν:E^w→F representing A and B, respectively, the Plücker flag relations ∑_{k=1}^{w+1} ε^k ν(y_1,...,y_k omitted,...,y_{w+1}) μ(y_k,x_1,...,x_{r-1}) ∈ N_F hold for all y_1,...,y_{w+1},x_1,...,x_{r-1}∈E, where ε is the distinguished element of F with 1+ε∈N_F. Then the following are equivalent: (1) (M_1,...,M_s) is a flag F-matroid, i.e. M_j ։ M_i for every 1≤i<j≤s; (2) C^*(M_i)⊆V^*(M_j) for every 1≤i<j≤s. Equivalently, for two F-matroids A and B on E, B ։ A if and only if C^*(A)⊆V^*(B).
proof_sketch: It suffices to prove the two-matroid assertion: for A of rank r with Plücker function μ and B of rank w with Plücker function ν, B ։ A iff C^*(A)⊆V^*(B). First assume B ։ A. To show a cocircuit Z of A is a covector of B, take an arbitrary circuit Y of B and prove Y·Z∈N_F. Choose z_0 in supp(Z) and a basis D={z_1,...,z_{n-r}} of A^* containing supp(Z)−z_0, so Z(z_i)=α ε^i μ^*(z_0,...,z_i omitted,...,z_{n-r}) for some α∈F^×. Let {x_1,...,x_{r-1}}=E−{z_0,...,z_{n-r}}. Likewise choose y_0 in supp(Y) and a basis {y_1,...,y_w} of B containing supp(Y)−y_0, so Y(y_j)=λ ε^j ν(y_0,...,y_j omitted,...,y_w). Let S={z_0,...,z_{n-r}}∩{y_0,...,y_w}, and match common elements by a bijection b with y_j=z_{b(j)}. Using the dual Plücker formula for μ^*, the sign identity obtained by moving z_{b(j)} past the other z's, and the vanishing of μ(y_j,x_1,...,x_{r-1}) outside S, one obtains a unit multiple of Y·Z equal to ∑_{j=0}^w ε^j ν(y_0,...,y_j omitted,...,y_w) μ(y_j,x_1,...,x_{r-1}). This is exactly a Plücker flag relation, hence lies in N_F; closure of N_F under scaling by units gives Y·Z∈N_F. Conversely assume C^*(A)⊆V^*(B). For arbitrary y_0,...,y_w and x_1,...,x_{r-1}, if there is no index ℓ such that {y_ℓ,x_1,...,x_{r-1}} is a basis of A and {y_0,...,y_ℓ omitted,...,y_w} is a basis of B, all terms in the desired flag relation vanish. Otherwise fix such an ℓ, put z_0=y_ℓ and {z_1,...,z_{n-r}}=E−{y_ℓ,x_1,...,x_{r-1}}, which is a basis of A^*. Define H∈C(A^*) by the usual circuit formula from μ^* on z_0,...,z_{n-r}, and define G∈C(B) by the usual circuit formula from ν on y_0,...,y_w. Since H∈C^*(A)⊆V^*(B), G·H∈N_F. The same intersection matching and sign computation as above identifies the Plücker flag sum with a unit multiple of G·H, so the relation holds. Applying this pairwise to all i<j proves the sequence version.

statement: [arXiv:2204.04658 Theorem 2.17] Let F be a tract and let M_1,...,M_s be F-matroids on the same finite ground set E. For an F-matroid P, let V(P)=C^*(P)^⊥ be its set of F-vectors and V^*(P)=C(P)^⊥ its set of F-covectors. Consider the following three properties: (1) the covector sets form a chain V^*(M_1)⊆V^*(M_2)⊆...⊆V^*(M_s); (2) (M_1,...,M_s) is a flag F-matroid, i.e. M_j ։ M_i for every 1≤i<j≤s; (3) the adjacent quotients hold, i.e. M_{i+1} ։ M_i for every 1≤i≤s−1. Then (1) implies (2), and (2) implies (3), for every tract F. If F is perfect, meaning that V(P) is orthogonal to V^*(P) for every F-matroid P, then (3) also implies (1). Thus over a perfect tract these three conditions are equivalent.
proof_sketch: The implication (2)⇒(3) is immediate by restricting the defining pairwise quotient conditions of a flag F-matroid to adjacent pairs. For (1)⇒(2), use the general orthogonality of circuits and cocircuits: C^*(M_i)⊆V^*(M_i). If V^*(M_i)⊆V^*(M_j) for i<j, then C^*(M_i)⊆V^*(M_j), and Theorem 2.4 gives M_j ։ M_i. For the converse under perfectness, assume only adjacent quotients M_{i+1} ։ M_i. Duality of quotients gives M_i^* ։ M_{i+1}^*; equivalently by Theorem 2.4, C^*(M_{i+1}^*)⊆V^*(M_i^*). Taking orthogonal complements reverses inclusions. Perfectness is used to identify V^*(M_i) with V^*(M_i^*)^⊥: indeed V^*(M_i^*)=V(M_i), and for perfect tracts the general inclusion V(M_i)^⊥⊆V^*(M_i) is an equality because V(M_i)⊥V^*(M_i). Therefore V^*(M_i)=V^*(M_i^*)^⊥⊆C^*(M_{i+1}^*)^⊥=V(M_{i+1}^*)=V^*(M_{i+1}). This proves each adjacent covector inclusion, hence the whole chain. The paper later applies this with F equal to the tropical hyperfield T, which is perfect, so chains of covector sets are exactly chains of tropical linear spaces.

statement: [arXiv:2204.04658 Theorem 3.12] Let E={1,...,n}, let r=(r_1,...,r_s) with 0≤r_1≤...≤r_s≤n, and set r^∨=(n−r_s,...,n−r_1). Let Fl(r,E) be the flag variety over F_1^± representing flag matroid bundles of rank r on E. There is a canonical isomorphism δ_{r,E}:Fl(r,E)→Fl(r^∨,E). It is induced from the usual complement-duality isomorphisms on Grassmannian factors: for the source factor Gr(r_i,E) and target factor Gr(n−r_i,E), if E={x_1,...,x_{n-r_i},x'_1,...,x'_{r_i}}, then the pullback of the target Plücker coordinate T_{x_1,...,x_{n-r_i}} is sign(x_1,...,x_{n-r_i},x'_1,...,x'_{r_i})·T_{x'_1,...,x'_{r_i}}, with the factors reversed so that the target type is r^∨. If F is an idyll equipped with an involution τ and M=(M_1,...,M_s) is a flag F-matroid with characteristic morphism χ_M:Spec F→Fl(r,E), then the dual flag F-matroid is M^*=(M_s^*,...,M_1^*) and its characteristic morphism is χ_{M^*}=δ_{r,E}∘χ_M∘τ^*:Spec F→Fl(r^∨,E), where τ^*:Spec F→Spec F is induced by τ.
proof_sketch: Start with the product of Grassmannians ∏_i Gr(r_i,E). The standard Grassmannian duality maps each factor to Gr(n−r_i,E) by replacing a Plücker coordinate with the complementary Plücker coordinate, up to the sign of the induced ordering of E; reverse the factors to obtain a map to the product whose rank sequence is r^∨. The only point is to check that this product map carries the closed subscheme Fl(r,E), defined by the Plücker flag relations, isomorphically onto Fl(r^∨,E). Consider a generating flag relation 0≤∑_{k=1}^{r_j+1} ε^k T_i(y_k,x_1,...,x_{r_i-1}) T_j(y_1,...,y_k omitted,...,y_{r_j+1}). Relations with repeated entries are already zero by alternating relations, so assume the x's and y's are distinct and complete them to complementary lists E={x_1,...,x_{r_i-1},x'_1,...,x'_{n-r_i+1}}={y_1,...,y_{r_j+1},y'_1,...,y'_{n-r_j-1}}. Applying the complement-coordinate formula to the terms transforms the relation, up to one common sign factor depending only on the chosen order of E, into the corresponding Plücker flag relation for the reversed dual ranks, namely a relation of the form 0≤∑ ε^k T_{dual j}(x'_k,y'_1,...,y'_{n-r_j-1}) T_{dual i}(x'_1,...,x'_k omitted,...,x'_{n-r_i+1}). Multiplication by a common unit sign is harmless in the ordered blueprint setting. Hence the image lies in Fl(r^∨,E), and by the same argument applied in the reverse direction the restriction is an isomorphism. For the statement on F-rational points, write the Plücker functions of M as φ_i(I)=χ_M^*(T_{i,I}). The dual Grassmann-Plücker functions satisfy, for complementary tuples I and I^c, φ_i^*(I^c)=sign(I^c,I)·τ(φ_i(I)), up to the same global scalar ambiguity as usual. This is exactly the pullback formula for δ_{r,E} composed with τ^*. The moduli property of Fl(r,E), equivalently uniqueness of characteristic morphisms for a given flag matroid bundle, then gives χ_{M^*}=δ_{r,E}∘χ_M∘τ^*.

statement: Literature fact to use: Fu--Tang--Wang, Adjoints of Matroids, Proposition 4.1: for simple M, M is modular iff it has an adjoint adM with L(adM) isomorphic to L(M)^op.
Literature fact to use: Fu--Tang--Wang, Theorem 4.3 and Proposition 4.4: modular simple matroids have unique adjoints and every connected component is either U_{1,1} or a finite projective geometry.

Literature fact to use: Wang, Lorentzian polynomials and tropical incidence, Proposition 4.14(3): if M is modular and W is its adjoint, then L(M) ≅ Qt^c_1(M) ≅ L(W)^op and in particular Dr^s_1(M) ≅ Trop(M).
Literature fact to use: Wang, Theorem 4.7: Dr^s_1(M)^o is the order-complex fan of chains of linear subclasses of M.

value: Need to know whether Dr(M) is genuinely unchanged by simplification, including loops and parallel classes, or only after imposing coordinate equalities.
Need incidence symmetry for the codimension-one polarity: p lies in alpha(q) iff q lies in alpha(p). Without this, even rank 3 may fail.

Prove the modular/simplification reduction and construct the explicit codimension-one polarity on cones; then decide whether rank <= 3 is solved and what extra lemma is needed for higher-rank polar duality.

note: Verified the key external facts: opposite flat lattice forces modularity after simplification; modular simple matroids decompose into rank-one/free components and finite projective geometries; and for modular M, Dr1_s(M) is canonically modeled by Trop(M) via elementary quotients/linear subclasses. This round assigns one solver to make the structural reduction precise, and one solver to build/test the codimension-one polarity and rank-3 case.

statement: An order-reversing involution of L(M) induces an order-reversing involution of L(si(M)), with the loop set cl_M(empty) as the bottom flat of L(M).
proof_sketch: Flats of M are exactly the loop set together with unions of parallel classes, giving a canonical lattice isomorphism L(M) ≅ L(si(M)); conjugating the involution by this isomorphism gives the induced involution.

statement: si(M) is modular.
proof_sketch: The induced anti-involution gives L(si(M)) ≅ L(si(M))^op, so si(M) is an adjoint of itself; by black-box fact A1, si(M) is modular.

statement: The connected components of si(M) are U_{1,1} components and finite projective geometries, and the involution permutes components by dualities between matching component types.
proof_sketch: By A3 the components have the stated form. Since atoms map to coatoms, projective-line intervals with at least three atoms force atoms from one component to map to coatoms in one component, giving an involutive component permutation and anti-isomorphisms of component lattices.

statement: The diagonal coordinate map induces a poset isomorphism Dr(si(M)) ≅ Dr(M).
proof_sketch: Loops force infinity and original parallel classes force equal coordinates. Any valuated quotient inside Trop(M) keeps original loops as loops and does not split original parallel classes; internal offsets must be zero, so the quotient descends uniquely to si(M), and conversely every quotient of si(M) has a zero parallel extension to M.

statement: The embedded flat subposet F ↦ Trop(M/F ⊕ U_{0,F}) is compatible with simplification and the prescribed involution.
proof_sketch: For F corresponding to Fbar in si(M), the matroid M/F ⊕ U_{0,F} is the zero parallel extension of si(M)/Fbar ⊕ U_{0,Fbar}; hence their tropical spaces correspond under the same diagonal embedding.

approach: Naive componentwise factorization of Dr for direct sums
reason: This fails because quotients can mix components; for example U_{2,2}=U_{1,1}⊕U_{1,1} has quotient U_{1,2}.

approach: Exact preservation of all parallel classes in quotients
reason: A quotient may turn an entire original parallel class into loops or create new parallelisms between distinct original classes; only non-splitting and zero internal offsets are forced.

statement: For a simple modular matroid with a flat-lattice polarity, the map alpha on codimension-one Dressian coordinates is w_H = q_{H^perp}; on rays it sends e_F to e_{H_{F^perp}} and is a fan isomorphism.
proof_sketch: The polarity reverses inclusions and the assignment G -> H_G also reverses inclusions, so their composition preserves chains. The inverse sends the ray indexed by H_G to e_{G^perp}.

statement: Alpha sends the flat point corresponding to a hyperplane H to the codimension-one flat subspace corresponding to H^perp, and sends the ray indexed by any flat F to the principal codimension-one ray indexed by F^perp.
proof_sketch: The coordinate test gives alpha(e_H) = e_{H_{H^perp}}. Fact B2 identifies H_{H^perp} with the principal elementary quotient associated to H^perp.

statement: Point-hyperplane incidence symmetry p in alpha(q) iff q in alpha(p) holds for all ranks under the simple modular polarity assumptions.
proof_sketch: Incidence reduces to basis equations min_i(p_{b_i}+q_{b_i*}) attained twice. Modularity implies B -> B* is an involution on bases, so these equations are identical after swapping p and q.

statement: For rank at most 3, the formulas Phi(point)=alpha(point), Phi(hyperplane)=alpha^{-1}(hyperplane), and Phi(bottom/top)=top/bottom define an order-reversing involution extending the flat polarity.
proof_sketch: The only nontrivial incidence is point contained in hyperplane, and that is reversed exactly by the proven incidence symmetry. Flat points and flat hyperplanes are matched by the ray computation.

approach: Testing U_{3,n} for n > 3
reason: The setup fails because U_{3,n} is not modular and has no order-reversing flat-lattice involution: it has n atoms but binomial(n,2) hyperplanes.

approach: Naive all-rank polar by ordinary intersection of alpha-hyperplanes
reason: Ordinary tropical intersections may have the wrong dimension or fail to be tropical linear spaces, so a stable-intersection or flag-completion theorem is needed.

statement: A tropical linear space determines its valuated Plücker vector up to a global additive scalar, so Dr(M) may be treated as a projectivized set of valuated matroid quotients when constructing maps.
proof_sketch: Fink and Moci state that L(p)=L(q) iff p and q differ by a global additive, tropically multiplicative, constant. This removes ambiguity in defining Phi by Plücker coordinates. ([cris.unibo.it](https://cris.unibo.it/retrieve/handle/11585/676967/e1dcb339-cf40-7715-e053-1705fe0a6cc9/adv2moci-fink.pdf?utm_source=openai))

statement: Flat-constancy lemma for relative quotients: If p is a rank-k valuated matroid quotient of an ordinary matroid M, then p_I depends only on the flat cl_M(I), for all finite p-bases I.
proof_sketch: The flag incidence relation between p and the constant valuated matroid M implies: for every circuit C of M and every subset A of size k-1, the minimum of p_{A∪{c}} over c in C minus A is attained at least twice, with invalid terms read as infinity. If two M-bases A∪{a} and A∪{b} span the same rank-k flat, then there is an M-circuit C contained in A∪{a,b} with C minus A = {a,b}; hence the two-term minimum forces p_{A∪{a}}=p_{A∪{b}}. The basis graph of the restriction of M to a flat is connected, so all p-bases spanning the same flat have the same value, and either every M-basis of that flat is a p-basis or none is.

value: Need the local dual Plücker lemma: for a flat-valued rank-k quotient p and four hyperplanes H1,H2,H3,H4 in a rank k+2 flat Y of a modular lattice, the three opposite-pair sums p(H1∩H2)+p(H3∩H4), p(H1∩H3)+p(H2∩H4), p(H1∩H4)+p(H2∩H3) must satisfy the tropical minimum-attained-twice relation.
Need the dual incidence lemma for the polar p^perp to remain a quotient of M: circuit equations of M for p^perp should translate to codimension-one quotient equations for p on rank k+1 intervals.
Need to prove flag/order reversal: if Trop(p) is contained in Trop(q), equivalently p is a quotient of q, then the polar construction should give Trop(q^perp) contained in Trop(p^perp).
Prove the all-rank flat-coordinate polarity theorem p -> p^perp and its flag/order-reversing property for simple modular M.

note: The promising route is no longer the naive intersection of polar hyperplanes, but a Plücker-coordinate construction on flats. We now have a likely all-rank formula: for a rank-k quotient p, define p^perp(J) = p(cl_M(J)^perp) for independent J of size r-k. Prove local dual Plücker and flag-incidence relations in modular intervals; if this works, it gives the desired order-reversing involution.

statement: Rank-at-most-three case for simple modular matroids with a flat-lattice polarity
For a valuated quotient p of M, the coordinates p_I are constant on all M-independent k-subsets I with the same M-closure.
proof_sketch: Flag incidence with the constant matroid M is equivalent to circuit-incidence over every M-circuit C. Circuit-incidence kills M-dependent k-sets and equates adjacent bases of the same flat; basis-graph connectivity gives flat-constancy.

statement: The coordinate formula p^perp_J = p_bar(cl_M(J)^perp) for M-independent J, and infinity otherwise, satisfies the tropical Plucker relations.
proof_sketch: A Plucker relation for p^perp localizes to four hyperplanes in a rank k+2 interval. If their common meet has rank k-2, it is an ordinary Plucker relation for p; if rank k-1, it is the rank-three codimension-one polarity relation; if rank k, it is trivial.

statement: The constructed p^perp is a valuated quotient of M.
proof_sketch: For A of size r-k-1, set X=cl(A) and Y=X^perp. The hyperplane restriction of p to Y polarizes, via the anti-isomorphism [X,E] -> [0,Y], to a tropical point of M/X; its circuit relations are exactly the circuit-incidence relations required for p^perp.

statement: The construction is involutive and extends the prescribed flat-lattice polarity on the embedded flat subposet.
proof_sketch: Flat-constancy gives (p^perp)^perp_I = p_bar((cl(I)^perp)^perp)=p_I. For a flat quotient M/F with loops on F, finite flats are complements of F; polarity sends complements of F to complements of F^perp.

approach: Direct proof of full mixed-rank order reversal
reason: For a general flag p quotient q, the incidence relation for q^perp quotient p^perp involves arbitrary A and B not adapted to a single modular interval, and the local Plucker/codimension-one arguments do not yet cover this mixed-rank case.

approach: Point-polar characterization x in Trop(p^perp) iff Trop(p) is contained in alpha(x)
reason: This would imply order reversal formally, but it is itself a special case of the missing mixed-rank flag-duality lemma.

statement: The local hyperplane lemma is true under the stated assumptions, with p(F)=infinity for flats not of rank k.
proof_sketch: If hyperplanes repeat, two sums are identical. Otherwise modularity reduces the total meet to codimension 2, 3, or 4. Codimension 2 is trivial, codimension 4 is the ordinary Plucker relation after using the four triple meets, and codimension 3 reduces by contraction to a rank-three edge lemma.

statement: The rank-three edge lemma holds for any loopless simple modular rank-three matroid and any w in its Bergman fan.
proof_sketch: Using the fine Bergman fan description, w is a nonnegative extended combination of indicators of a point-line flag. The opposite-edge sums become weighted matching costs for four colored objects, and an elementary case check shows the minimum is never unique.

statement: In rank 4 and k=2, four planes in PG(3,q), including PG(3,2), satisfy the tropical opposite-edge relation, with all degeneracies covered.
proof_sketch: No common point gives an ordinary tetrahedral Plucker relation; common point gives the rank-three edge lemma after contraction; common line and repeated planes are trivial.

statement: For rank 4, the lemma verifies the middle-rank Plucker axiom for the polar rank-two valuation p_perp(L)=p(L_perp).
proof_sketch: The Plucker relation for p_perp on four points a_i becomes the local lemma for p applied to the four polar hyperplanes a_i_perp.

approach: Search for ordinary counterexamples in PG(3,2) or projective-plane-plus-point direct sums
reason: All configurations fall into the modular codimension 2, 3, or 4 cases; attempted one-finite-term failures are blocked by the rank-three matching/flag argument.

approach: Handle the codimension-three case by ordinary Plucker over the total meet
reason: The total meet has rank k-1, so only a rank-one localization remains; the needed input is the quotient circuit-incidence relation, not an ordinary four-element Plucker relation.

value: Need to extend the modular dual-basis incidence argument from adapted configurations to arbitrary subsets A and B in the mixed-rank polar flag relation.
Need to prove or disprove the point-annihilator characterization: x in Trop(p^dagger) iff Trop(p) is contained in the polar hyperplane alpha(x).
Prove the mixed-rank flag-duality lemma: for simple modular M with flat-lattice polarity, p ↞ q should imply q^dagger ↞ p^dagger.

note: The construction p^dagger is no longer the issue; it is well-defined, involutive, and flat-compatible. Focus all remaining effort on order reversal. Two promising routes are direct mixed flag incidence and the point-polar annihilator characterization.

statement: For a finite simple modular matroid M with a flat-lattice polarity, the flat-coordinate construction p -> p^dagger gives a well-defined rank-reversing involution on the underlying set of relative Dressian points and extends the prescribed polarity on the embedded flat subposet.
Point-annihilator theorem: for every tropical point x in Trop(M), x is in Trop(p^dagger) if and only if Trop(p) is contained in Trop(alpha(x)).
proof_sketch: Restrict to each Boolean apartment B of M. On the polar apartment B^star, p^dagger restricts to the ordinary dual of p restricted to B, so ordinary valuated-matroid annihilator duality converts the point equations for p^dagger into the hyperplane-containment equations for alpha(x).

statement: Consequently Phi(Trop(p)) = Trop(p^dagger) is an order-reversing involution on Dr(M) extending the flat-lattice polarity.
proof_sketch: By the point-annihilator theorem, Phi(Trop(p)) is exactly the set of points whose alpha-hyperplanes contain Trop(p). Inclusion reverses immediately for such annihilator sets, while involutivity and flat-extension are the coordinate facts from t3.

approach: Directly match every mixed-rank flag equation to one point equation
reason: For non-adapted bases the terms do not form a single p^dagger circuit equation; the correct replacement is ordinary duality on each Boolean apartment.

statement: For a simple modular matroid M with a flat-lattice polarity, the flat-coordinate construction p -> p^dagger is an involution on relative Dressian points and extends the prescribed polarity on embedded flats.

statement: Point-annihilator theorem: for every tropical point x of Trop(M), x is in Trop(p^dagger) if and only if Trop(p) is contained in the polar hyperplane Trop(alpha(x)).
proof_sketch: On any Boolean apartment B of M, the polar apartment B* is again a basis apartment and p^dagger restricted to B* is the ordinary valuated matroid dual of p restricted to B. Ordinary tropical annihilator duality on the Boolean apartment translates the point equations for p^dagger into the hyperplane-containment equations for alpha(x).

statement: Consequently, Phi(Trop(p)) = Trop(p^dagger) is an order-reversing involution on Dr(M).
proof_sketch: The point-annihilator theorem identifies Phi(L) with the set of points whose polar hyperplanes contain L. statement: The mixed-rank flag-duality lemma also holds: if p is a valuated quotient of q, then q^dagger is a valuated quotient of p^dagger.

The heart of the argument is two key lemmas—a local rank-three dual Plücker matching (Lemma 5.1) and a codimension-one polarity lemma for circuits (Lemma 7.1)—which together show these minima always tie, so p† is again a valuated quotient; involutivity then follows immediately because taking orthogonal complements twice returns the original flat.
